request.question: Noam Jenkins had a role in the 2014 video game developed by which company?

title: Watch Dogs
Godzilla (2014 video game)
Lost Dimension
Sentinels of the Multiverse: The Video Game
Jet Car Stunts
The Evil Within 2
Sometimes Always Monsters
Fossil Fighters: Frontier
Noam Jenkins
Lord of Magna: Maiden Heaven
sentences: Watch Dogs (stylized as WATCH_DOGS) is an action-adventure video game developed by Ubisoft Montreal and published by Ubisoft.
It was released worldwide on 27 May 2014 for Microsoft Windows, PlayStation 3, PlayStation 4, Xbox 360 and Xbox One, while a Wii U version was released in November 2014.
Set within a fictionalized version of Chicago, Illinois, the single-player story follows a hacker and his efforts to seek revenge after the killing of his niece.
The open world design lets players freely roam the urban city, suburbs, open countryside, and run-down neighborhoods of Chicago.

Godzilla (also Godzilla: The Game, ゴジラ Godzilla, Gojira Godzilla, and Godzilla VS for the PS4 in Japan) is a 2014 video game developed by Natsume and published by Bandai Namco Games for the PlayStation 3 and PlayStation 4 based on the Japanese monster "Godzilla" franchise by Toho.
It was first released on December 18, 2014 in Japan only for the PlayStation 3.
It was released on July 14, 2015 in North America and on July 17, 2015 in Europe.
The Western PlayStation 4 version is based on the upgraded Japanese release called "Godzilla VS", released on July 14, 2015, containing more content such as additional monsters.
Lost Dimension (ロストディメンション , Rosuto Dimenshon ) is a 2014 video game developed by Lancarse originally for the PlayStation 3 and PlayStation Vita.
It is a tactical role-playing video game with visual novel-like elements, where the choices of the player determine the fate of the game's story, as well as its characters.
The game's protagonist is 18-year-old Sho Kasugai, a member of S.E.A.L.E.D., an organisation tasked with stopping a mysterious man named "The End", who wants to destroy the world.
A Microsoft Windows version, published by Ghostlight, will be released in 2017.
Sentinels of the Multiverse: The Video Game is a 2014 video game developed and published by Handelabra Games.
It is an adaptation of the board game of the same name.
The game was released for iOS and Android on October 15, 2014 and for Steam on December 22, 2014.
Jet Car Stunts is a 2014 video game developed by Grip Games and published by BitComposer Entertainment.
It is a remake of the 2009 iOS game of the same name.
The Evil Within 2 is an upcoming third-person survival horror video game developed by Tango Gameworks and published by Bethesda Softworks for Microsoft Windows, PlayStation 4 and Xbox One.
It is the sequel to the 2014 video game "The Evil Within".
Sometimes Always Monsters is an upcoming role-playing video game developed by Vagabond Dog and published by Devolver Digital.
It is the sequel to the well-received 2014 video game "Always Sometimes Monsters".
Fossil Fighters: Frontier, known in Japan as Kasekihorider Mugengear (カセキホリダー ムゲンギア , lit.
"Fossil Diggers Infinite Gear" ) , is a 2014 video game developed by Spike Chunsoft, with assistance from Red Entertainment and Cyclone Zero, and published by Nintendo for the Nintendo 3DS console.
The game is the third title in the "Fossil Fighters" series, and the first on the Nintendo 3DS.

It was released in Japan on February 27, 2014, in North America on March 20, 2015, and on May 29, 2015 in Europe, making it the first installment of the series to be officially released in that part of the world.
Noam Jenkins is a Canadian actor, voice actor, and television director.
He is best known for his portrayal as Aiden Pearce in the 2014 video game "Watch Dogs", and Detective Jerry Barber on Global's "Rookie Blue".
Lord of Magna: Maiden Heaven is a 2014 video game developed by Marvelous for the Nintendo 3DS handheld video game console.
The game was released in Japan in October 2014, and was released worldwide in June 2015 by XSEED Games.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Noam Jenkins had a role in the 2014 video game developed by which company?
hop1_docs: Noam Jenkins | Noam Jenkins is a Canadian actor, voice actor, and television director.  He is best known for his portrayal as Aiden Pearce in the 2014 video game "Watch Dogs", and Detective Jerry Barber on Global's "Rookie Blue".

Neverland (company) | Neverland Company Inc. (株式会社ネバーランドカンパニー , Kabushiki-gaisha Nabārando Kanpanī ) was a Japanese video game developer founded on May 7, 1993.  It has developed games for Super NES, Dreamcast, GameCube, PlayStation 2, Game Boy Color, Nintendo DS, Nintendo 3DS, PlayStation Portable, PlayStation 3 and the Wii.  The most notable games this company developed were part of the "Lufia" and "Rune Factory" series of video games.  On November 29, 2013, the company announced that it would cease operations and file for bankruptcy.  The very next day, "Rune Factory" producer Yoshifumi Hashimoto of Marvelous AQL, the series publisher, said that while what their next project was a secret, "the staff members are still doing great."  In February 2014, it was revealed that the "Rune Factory" development team had been hired by Marvelous AQL, and that they were developing the Nintendo 3DS simulation role-playing video game "".

Infamous Quests | Infamous Quests is an indie video game development company that produces point and click adventure games.  It was founded in 2012 by Steven Alexander and Shawn Mills who previously founded Infamous Adventures, an amateur game development company that remade old Sierra Entertainment adventure games of the early 1990s.  The two chose to form Infamous Quests in order to separate it from their free fan-remake development company and in 2012 they announced "Quest for Infamy", an adventure RPG made with Adventure Game Studio where the player assumes the role of the morally gray William Roehm.  The developers raised $63,281 on Kickstarter to produce "Quest For Infamy", and in June 2014 the game made Time Magazine's "20 Video Games To Watch for Summer 2014" list.

En Masse Entertainment | En Masse Entertainment, Inc. (formerly Bluehole Interactive, Inc.) is an American video game publisher and a subsidiary of South Korean video game developer Bluehole.  Founded in June 2008, the company is based in Seattle, Washington and is best-known for publishing massively multiplayer online role-playing game "TERA", which was developed by parent company Bluehole.  The original En Masse leadership team had extensive experience in the video game industry at leading companies, including Blizzard Entertainment, Microsoft, NCsoft, ArenaNet, and Electronic Arts.  In January 2013, Chris Lee and Soo Min Park became chief executive officer and chief operating officer, respectively.

Warhorse Studios | Warhorse Studios s.r.o. (formerly Prague Game Studios s.r.o.) is a Czech video game developer based in Prague, Czech Republic.  It was founded on 31 August 2011 by Daniel Vávra, former writer and game designer of 2K Czech, and Martin Klíma, former producer of Altar Games, Codemasters, and Bohemia Interactive.  Originally, the company held the name Prague Game Studios s.r.o., only adopting the trademark "Warhorse Studios", but was renamed to Warhorse Studios s.r.o. on 8 July 2014.  The studio is currently developing role-playing video game "", which is scheduled to release in February 2018.

List of Square Enix video game franchises | Square Enix is a Japanese video game development and publishing company formed from the merger of video game developer Square and publisher Enix on April 1, 2003.  The company is best known for its role-playing video game franchises, which include the "Final Fantasy" series, the "Dragon Quest" series, and the action role-playing "Kingdom Hearts" series.  Since its inception, the company has developed or published hundreds of titles in various video game franchises on numerous gaming systems.  Of its properties, the "Final Fantasy" franchise is the best-selling, with a total worldwide sales of over 110 million units.  The "Dragon Quest" series has shipped over 66 million units worldwide and is one of the most popular video game series in Japan, while the "Kingdom Hearts" series has shipped over 22 million copies worldwide.

Munin (video game) | Munin is an independent video game which was published by Daedalic Entertainment on June 10, 2014.  The game was developed by game company Gojira.  The game is a puzzle-platformer, with references to Norse gods and monsters.  The iOS version of the game was released on July 7, 2014.
hop1_titles: List of Square Enix video game franchises
response.summary_1: Passage [1]: Noam Jenkins "is best known for his portrayal as Aiden Pearce in the video game 'Watch Dogs'." Missing fact/bridge: the developer of the video game "Watch Dogs" (i.e., which company developed "Watch Dogs"). Retrieval direction/anchors: search for the developer of "Watch Dogs" (use quoted anchors "Aiden Pearce" and "Watch Dogs" from Passage [1] or queries like "Watch Dogs developer" / "Who developed Watch Dogs?").
hop2_docs: Noam Jenkins | Noam Jenkins is a Canadian actor, voice actor, and television director.  He is best known for his portrayal as Aiden Pearce in the 2014 video game "Watch Dogs", and Detective Jerry Barber on Global's "Rookie Blue".

Watch Dogs 2 | Watch Dogs 2 (stylized as WATCH_DOGS 2) is an action-adventure video game developed by Ubisoft Montreal and published by Ubisoft.  It is the sequel to 2014's "Watch Dogs" and was released worldwide for PlayStation 4, Xbox One and Microsoft Windows in November 2016.

Dog watch | Dog watch, in marine or naval terminology, is a watch, a period of work duty or a work shift, between 16:00 and 20:00 (4 pm and 8 pm).  This period is split into two, with the 'first' dog watch from 16:00 to 18:00 (4 pm to 6 pm) and the 'last' dog watch from 18:00 to 20:00 (6 pm to 8 pm).  Each of these watches is half the length of a standard watch.

Bucovina Shepherd Dog | The Bucovina Shepherd (Câine Ciobănesc de Bucovina) is one of the largest and most powerful, rustic livestock guardian dogs, with strong watch dog qualities, which was developed over many centuries by shepherds in the Carpathian Mountains.

Guard dog | A guard dog or watch dog (not to be confused with the attack dog) is a dog used to guard against, and watch for unwanted or unexpected people or animals.  The dog is discriminating so that it does not annoy or attack familiar people.

Cultural depictions of the dog | Cultural depictions of dogs in Western art extend back thousands of years to when dogs were portrayed on the walls of caves.  Representations of dogs in art became more elaborate as individual breeds evolved and the relationships between human and canine developed.  Hunting scenes were popular in the Middle Ages and the Renaissance.  Dogs were depicted to symbolize guidance, protection, loyalty, fidelity, faithfulness, watchfulness, and love.
Dog Watch | Dog Watch was a direct-to-video film released in 1996.
response.summary_2: Noam Jenkins portrayed Aiden Pearce in the 2014 game Watch Dogs; Watch Dogs 2 is credited as "developed by Ubisoft Montreal" and is the sequel to 2014's Watch Dogs, supporting that the 2014 game was developed by Ubisoft Montreal.
